# Create scopes to group resources

Scopes are groupings of resources.
You create scopes to partition resources and then assign ownership of those resources to users and groups.
You can think of scopes as permission boundaries, modeled as containers.

There are three types of scopes in Boundary, they have the following hierarchy:

1. Global

  There is only one global scope.
  It is the outermost container.
  You perform the initial administration and setup in the global scope.
  You also use the global scope to manage any org scopes.
1. Org

  You can create multiple org scopes under the global scope.
  Org scopes contain identity and access management-related resources and project scopes.
1. Project

  You can create multiple project scopes under an org scope.
  Project scopes contain infrastructure-related resources.

You can associate some resources only with a specific level of scope.
For example, you can create targets only in a project scope.
Other resources can exist in multiple scopes.
You can create users in either the global scope or an org scope, depending on the resources you want them to access.

For specific information about Boundary resources and their valid scope types, refer to the [scopes](/boundary/docs/domain-model/scopes) domain model documentation.

## Create an org scope

Complete the following steps to create an org scope in the global scope:

<Tabs>
<Tab heading="UI" group="ui">

1. Log in to Boundary.
1. In the navigation menu, select **Orgs**.
1. Click **New** or **New Org**.
1. (Optional) Enter a name and description for the org scope.
1. Click **Save**.

The new org scope appears on the **Orgs** page.

</Tab>
<Tab heading="CLI" group="cli">

1. Log in to Boundary.
1. Enter the following command, and substitute the `name` and `description` you want to use for the org scope:

```shell-session
$ boundary scopes create -scope-id global -name <ORG_NAME> -description <ORG_DESCRIPTION>

Scope information:
  Created Time:        Tue, 24 Feb 2026 05:48:22 PDT
  Description:         org_description
  ID:                  o_y0fEd8iY2J
  Name:                org_name
  Updated Time:        Wed, 25 Feb 2026 05:48:22 PDT
  Version:             1

  Scope (parent):
    ID:                global
    Name:              global
    Type:              global
```

</Tab>
<Tab heading="Terraform" group="terraform">

You can create an org scope using the Terraform provider.

Unlike the CLI and UI, the Terraform provider does not create administrative roles for the scope by default.
To ensure the user who creates the scope can manage it immediately, set `auto_create_default_role` and `auto_create_admin_role` to `true`.

Enter the following command, and substitute the `name` and `description` you want to use for the org scope:

```hcl
resource "boundary_scope" "org" {
  scope_id                 = "global"
  name                     = "org-name"
  description              = "org-description"
  auto_create_default_role = true
  auto_create_admin_role   = true
}
```

</Tab>
</Tabs>

## Create a project scope

Complete the following steps to create a project scope in an org scope:

<Tabs>
<Tab heading="UI" group="ui">

1. Log in to Boundary.
1. In the navigation menu, select **Orgs**.
1. Select the name of the org you want to add a project scope to.
1. Click **New** or **New Project**.
1. (Optional) Enter a name and description for the project scope.
1. Click **Save**.

The new project scope appears on the **Projects** page.

</Tab>
<Tab heading="CLI" group="cli">

1. Log in to Boundary.
1. Enter the following command, and substitute the `name` and `description` you want to use for the project scope:

```shell-session
$ boundary scopes create -scope-id o_0MkQUfE9jA -name <PROJECT_NAME> -description <PROJECT_DESCRIPTION>

Scope information:
  Created Time:        Tue, 29 Sep 2020 05:57:45 PDT
  Description:         project_description
  ID:                  p_jqCwqjSTQ4
  Name:                project_name
  Updated Time:        Tue, 29 Sep 2020 05:57:45 PDT
  Version:             1

  Scope (parent):
    ID:                o_0MkQUfE9jA
    Name:              org-name
    Parent Scope ID:   global
    Type:              org
```

</Tab>
<Tab heading="Terraform" group="terraform">

You can create a project scope using the Terraform provider.

Unlike the CLI and UI, the Terraform provider does not create administrative roles for the scope by default.
To ensure the user who creates the scope can manage it immediately, set `auto_create_default_role` and `auto_create_admin_role` to `true`.

Enter the following command, and substitute the `name` and `description` you want to use for the project scope:

```hcl
resource "boundary_scope" "project" {
  name             = "project-name"
  description      = "project-description"

  # scope_id is taken from the parent org resource
  scope_id                 = boundary_scope.org.id
  auto_create_default_role = true
  auto_create_admin_role   = true
}
```

</Tab>
</Tabs>

## More information

- To better understand Boundary's permissions model, refer to [Permissions in Boundary](/boundary/docs/rbac).
- For more specific information about the scopes resource, refer to the [scopes domain model](/boundary/docs/domain-model/scopes) topic.
- To learn more about using the CLI to create or manage scopes, refer to the [`scopes`](/boundary/docs/commands/scopes) topic.
- To learn more about using the API to create or manage scopes, refer to the [scope service](/boundary/api-docs/scope-service) topic.

## Next steps

1. After you create scopes to contain your resources, you can [Manage access with roles](/boundary/docs/rbac/manage-roles).
1. To manage access to resources dynamically, refer to [Manage principals](/boundary/docs/rbac/users/manage-users-groups) and [Filter managed groups](/boundary/docs/rbac/users/managed-groups).
